15.text: Standard library for file path construction. Used to locate the shared dataset relative to the script.

16.text: Python's built-in random module, used here to shuffle the dataset into a random training order.

19.text: PyTorch's neural network building blocks: layers, modules, and everything needed to define model architecture.

20.text: Functional API for softmax, cross-entropy, etc.

22.text: Fix both Python and PyTorch random seeds so weight initialization and data ordering are identical across runs.

28.text: Dataset path, shared across all labs.

29.text: If the names dataset is not already on disk, download it automatically so the tutorial is self-contained.

32.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's GitHub repository.

34.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings. Each line becomes one training document.

35.text: Shuffle the names so the model encounters them in random order rather than memorizing alphabetical patterns.

38.text: Build the character-level vocabulary: 26 lowercase letters, each mapped to a unique integer ID.

39.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

40.text: The real vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 letters plus the BOS/EOS marker. PAD is separate and never predicted.

41.text: PAD token for batch alignment. Sequences in a batch have different lengths, so shorter ones get padded to match the longest.

42.text: Expanded vocab includes PAD, the embedding table needs a row for it, even though it's never a real prediction target.

48.text: 4x larger embedding dimension than lab 03. Batching amortizes the overhead, so we can afford a bigger model.

49.text: Same 4 attention heads, but now each has 16 dimensions instead of 4.

50.text: 2 layers instead of 1, deeper model for richer representations.

51.text: Same 16-character context window as previous labs. Names longer than this get truncated.

52.text: Each head now has 16 dimensions (64 / 4), four times larger than in lab 03. More dimensions per head means richer attention patterns.

53.text: 32 sequences per training step, the model sees 32 names simultaneously and averages their gradients.

54.text: 1000 steps, same as previous labs. But each step processes 32x more data.

60.text: RMSNorm, identical to lab 03. The architecture doesn't change, just the scale.

69.text: Attention module, now with a pad_mask parameter. This is the key addition for batching.

72.text: Same Q, K, V projections as lab 03.

73.text: Project the normalized input into key vectors that other positions will compare against.

74.text: Project the input into value vectors that carry content to be aggregated by attention weights.

77.text: Attention now accepts a padding mask, the model needs to know which positions are real vs. padding.

80.text: Project into key vectors and reshape into separate attention heads. Keys let each position advertise its content for query matching.

81.text: Project into value vectors and reshape into heads. Values carry the content that attention weights will blend together.

83.text: Compute attention scores by taking the dot product of queries and keys, scaled by the square root of head dimension to prevent saturation.

85.text: Causal mask: same as lab 03, prevents attending to future positions.

86.text: Set future positions to negative infinity so the model cannot cheat by looking ahead.

88.text: Padding mask: if provided, also prevent attending to PAD positions.

90.text: Expand pad_mask to (B, 1, 1, T) and fill PAD positions with -inf. Without this, the model would learn spurious patterns from padding tokens.

91.text: Turn raw attention scores into a probability distribution so weights sum to one across keys.

92.text: Safety net: when ALL keys are masked (a fully-padded row), softmax produces NaN. Replace with 0 so the model ignores those positions entirely.

95.text: Project the concatenated multi-head attention output back into the embedding space.

101.text: Widen the representation to 4x the embedding dimension for richer nonlinear transformations.

102.text: Compress the expanded hidden state back to the original embedding dimension.

104.text: Apply ReLU activation between two linear layers, forming the feed-forward sub-network.

105.text: The entire MLP compresses into a single chained expression in PyTorch.

108.text: Transformer block, now passes pad_mask through to attention.

111.text: Normalize the input before passing it to the attention sub-layer for stable training.

112.text: The attention sub-layer now receives the padding mask so it knows which positions hold real tokens versus filler.

113.text: Normalize the input before the MLP sub-layer, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

114.text: The feed-forward network that processes each position independently after attention mixing.

117.text: Attention receives the mask so it can ignore padded positions.

118.text: MLP doesn't need the mask, it operates position-independently.

122.text: Complete model, embedding table is now larger to accommodate the PAD token.

125.text: padding_idx=PAD tells the embedding to output zeros for PAD tokens and exclude them from gradient updates. A clean way to handle variable-length sequences.

127.text: Normalize embeddings before entering the transformer stack. This initial normalization keeps activations stable from the start.

128.text: Register all transformer layers so PyTorch can discover and optimize their parameters.

129.text: Output head maps to real vocab only (27 tokens, not 28). PAD is never a valid prediction.

130.text: Walk through every sub-module and apply N(0, 0.08) initialization, ensuring consistent starting conditions across all labs.

138.text: Unpack batch size B and sequence length T from the input shape. These dimensions drive all the downstream tensor operations.

139.text: Token embeddings, PAD tokens get zero vectors thanks to padding_idx.

140.text: Position embeddings broadcast across the batch.

141.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer stack.

142.text: Pass the representation through each transformer layer in sequence. With two layers now, the model builds progressively richer representations.

143.text: Each layer receives the padding mask.

144.text: Output logits over the real vocabulary.

150.text: Batch creation: the infrastructure needed for mini-batch training. This is where you see the engineering overhead of batching.

153.text: Gather batch_size documents, cycling through the dataset.

154.text: Select the document for this batch position.

159.text: Find the longest sequence in this batch, everything gets padded to this length.

160.text: Allocate lists that will hold the padded input tokens, shifted targets, and boolean masks for every sequence in the batch.

163.text: For each sequence, create aligned input, target, and mask.

164.text: Actual sequence length (minus 1 for the target shift).

166.text: Targets: shifted tokens + -100 fill. PyTorch's cross_entropy ignores positions marked -100.

167.text: Boolean mask: False for real tokens, True for padding.

173.text: The input tensor of shape (B, max_T) containing token IDs, with PAD filling shorter sequences to the batch's maximum length.

174.text: The target tensor of shape (B, max_T) with shifted tokens as labels. Padded positions use -100 so cross-entropy ignores them.

175.text: A boolean mask of shape (B, max_T) marking which positions are real tokens (False) and which are padding (True).

182.text: Run training on CPU, which is sufficient for this small model and dataset size.

183.text: Create the model with the scaled-up architecture: 64-dim embeddings, 4 heads, and 2 transformer layers.

184.text: Parameter count, much larger than lab 03 due to bigger dimensions and 2 layers.

186.text: Same Adam optimizer configuration as all previous labs: lr=0.01, betas=(0.85, 0.99). Only the model size and batch size changed.

188.text: Iterate over training steps, processing a batch of 32 names at each step.

189.text: Assemble a padded batch of 32 tokenized names, aligning them to the same length with PAD tokens and building the corresponding masks.

190.text: Transfer input token IDs to the compute device. On CPU this is a no-op, but the pattern generalizes to GPU training.

191.text: Transfer target labels to the compute device, matching where the model's output tensors will live.

192.text: Transfer the padding mask to the same device so it can be applied directly to the attention score matrix.

194.text: Forward pass with padding mask, the model knows which positions to ignore.

195.text: ignore_index=-100 means the loss is only computed on real tokens, not padding. This is crucial, otherwise shorter sequences would be penalized differently than longer ones.

197.text: Clear accumulated gradients from the previous step before computing new ones.

198.text: Backward pass, autograd computes gradients for all parameters through the batched forward pass.

199.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

200.text: Update learning rate for all parameter groups.

201.text: Write the decayed learning rate into the optimizer's parameter group.

202.text: Use the Adam optimizer to update all model weights based on the computed gradients.

204.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

210.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

212.text: Switch the model to evaluation mode, which disables training-specific behaviors like dropout.

214.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

215.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

217.text: Feed generated sequence as a batch of 1. No padding needed for single-sequence inference.

218.text: Forward pass, no pad_mask needed for single sequences.

219.text: Grab the logits from the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower temperature sharpens the distribution toward the top predictions.

220.text: Convert the temperature-scaled logits into a proper probability distribution that sums to one, ready for sampling.

221.text: Draw one token from the probability distribution to continue generating the name.

222.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it signals the name is complete and we stop adding characters.

224.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing sequence so it becomes context for predicting the next character.

225.text: Map each token ID back to its character. Skip the leading BOS to get just the generated name text.

226.text: Display the completed generated name after all characters have been sampled.
